retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Complications -- Overwhelming Post-Splenectomy Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who are hyposplenic might already be taking prophylactic antibiotics, and hence, cultures might be negative; however, antibiotic therapy should continue. Empiric parental antibiotic therapy should include vancomycin and either cefotaxime or ceftriaxone. Although the efficacy of intravenous immunoglobulin treatment for asplenic sepsis has not been proven, some experts recommend its administration in this situation. The mortality of OPSI is high, with overall mortality rates of 50% to 70%, and death may result in 24 to 48 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Splenomegaly -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who undergo splenectomy are at increased risk of infections secondary to encapsulated organisms such as Haemophilus Influenzae , Streptococcus pneumoniae , and Neisseria meningitidis. Vaccinations against these organisms are highly recommended in patients who have undergone splenectomy. Careful attention must be paid to post-splenectomy patients presenting with febrile illnesses as they may require more aggressive, empiric antibiotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Splenectomy -- Complications -- Early Postoperative Complications in the Three Months Following Splenectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Intrabdominal abscess: most commonly in the subphrenic space and secondary to an infected hematoma or gastrointestinal tract injury. Drainage and antibiotics are typically required. These abscesses are more common in patients with immune thrombocytopenic purpura for reasons that are not well understood. [9]

retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Injury(Archived) -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Post-splenectomy patients should receive vaccinations against encapsulated bacteria before hospital discharge. These vaccines include pneumococcal, Haemophilus influenza type B, and meningococcal series by day 14 or upon discharge. [26] Follow-up vaccinations for meningococcal are needed at 2-month intervals. Prophylactic antibiotics are also recommended. Children should receive penicillin V (250 mg/d) for at least 2 years, with life-long antibiotic therapy recommended for high-risk patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Asplenism -- Complications -- Overwhelming Post-Splenectomy Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) is a syndrome encountered in patients who have functional asplenia. Bacteremia caused by encapsulated microorganisms characterizes the syndrome, with the most commonly reported pathogen in OPSI being Streptococcus pneumoniae . The syndrome might present without a visible primary source of infection and with a short prodromal phase; clinical presentations include septic shock, disseminated intravascular coagulopathy, and bilateral adrenal hemorrhage. OPSI is a medical emergency, and antibiotics should be administered immediately, along with supportive measures. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Post a splenectomy, patients should receive vaccinations before discharge or within 2 weeks to lower the risk of sepsis. Common organisms include Streptococcus pneumoniae , Neisseria meningitidis , and Haemophilus influenzae type b. The 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) and others, such as PCV20 (Prevnar 20) and PedvaxHIB, are recommended. Despite widespread pediatric immunization, postsplenectomy recommendations remain unchanged. [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Splenectomy has associated intra- and postoperative complications; postoperative complications may occur early, in the initial 3 postoperative months, or later.

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Trauma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Splenectomy increases susceptibility to infections caused by encapsulated pathogens and parasites that reside within erythrocytes. Overwhelming postsplenectomy sepsis still carries a 50% mortality. [3] Patients are also at elevated risk for early and delayed infections and may have an increased risk for thromboembolic complications. The effective use of diagnostic tools and therapeutic strategies is key to improving outcomes and minimizing preventable morbidity and mortality. [2]

retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as a type of Physical Treatment Methods, placing it within the broader category of physical therapeutic interventions. While blood transfusion can be a valuable clinical procedure, it is also recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of its classification under physical treatment methods and its possible role in postoperative or procedural complications is essential for clinicians to manage risks and optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: Other nonoperative procedures are linked to blood transfusion, which is also referred to by its Arabic term نقل الدم. Blood transfusion (نقل الدم) is classified as a type of other nonoperative procedure, indicating that it falls within the broader category of interventions that do not involve operative surgery. Additionally, blood transfusion (نقل الدم) is recognized as a potential cause of complications arising from surgical procedures or other forms of medical care, highlighting the need for careful management and monitoring when this therapy is employed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341

Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.
